title: UCB — gMG — Knowledge Doc
html:
Market Context: RYSTIGGO (rozanolixizumab) + ZILBRYSQ (zilucoplan) in Generalized Myasthenia Gravis

Products: RYSTIGGO (rozanolixizumab-noli) + ZILBRYSQ (zilucoplan) Manufacturer: UCB S.A. Indication: Generalized myasthenia gravis (gMG) in adult patients Document scope: Dual-brand competitive/clinical landscape — disease, UCB portfolio, and competitive positioning Last updated: 2026-04-25

1. Disease Landscape

1.1 Epidemiology

Myasthenia Gravis (MG):

  • MG is a chronic autoimmune neuromuscular disorder characterized by fluctuating skeletal muscle weakness and fatigue
  • US prevalence: ~37 cases per 100,000 people (claims-based, 2016-2021 data), equating to ~120,000-150,000 patients in the US
  • Age- and sex-standardized incidence: ~68.5 new cases per million person-years in commercially/Medicare-insured US populations
  • Global prevalence estimates range from 20 to 475 per million, with a mean of ~173 per million; rising steadily due to improved diagnosis and aging populations
  • Bimodal age distribution: early peak in women (20-40 years) and late peak in men (60-80 years); elderly-onset MG is the fastest-growing demographic segment

Generalized MG (gMG):

  • ~50% of patients initially present with ocular symptoms (ptosis, diplopia); of these, ~50% progress to generalized disease within 2 years
  • gMG involves weakness of limb, bulbar (chewing, swallowing, speaking), and respiratory muscles
  • MGFA classification: Class I (ocular only) through Class V (intubation with or without mechanical ventilation)
  • The commercially relevant population for targeted biologics is Class II-IVa gMG — this is the population studied in pivotal trials

1.2 MG Subtypes by Autoantibody

MG is classified by the pathogenic autoantibody, which has direct implications for treatment eligibility:

Subtype Prevalence Target Approved Biologics
AChR Ab+ 80-85% of MG Acetylcholine receptor at the neuromuscular junction Vyvgart, Rystiggo, Zilbrysq, Soliris, Ultomiris, Imaavy, Uplizna
MuSK Ab+ 5-8% of MG Muscle-specific tyrosine kinase Vyvgart, Rystiggo, Imaavy, Uplizna
LRP4 Ab+ 1-2% of MG Lipoprotein-related protein 4 No approved biologics (cell-based assay detection only)
Seronegative 10-15% of MG Unknown / undetectable by standard assays No approved biologics (Vyvgart sBLA under FDA review, PDUFA May 2026)

Key implication for UCB: Rystiggo is approved for both AChR+ and MuSK+ gMG. Zilbrysq is approved only for AChR+ gMG. This means Rystiggo covers both major antibody subtypes, while Zilbrysq serves the larger AChR+ majority. UCB can offer mechanism choice to AChR+ patients and has a MuSK+ option that competitors like Zilbrysq and Soliris/Ultomiris cannot match.

1.3 Pathophysiology

AChR+ gMG: Autoantibodies (primarily IgG1 and IgG3) bind the acetylcholine receptor at the postsynaptic neuromuscular junction, causing:

  1. Complement-mediated destruction of the endplate (MAC formation)
  2. Accelerated internalization and degradation of AChR (antigenic modulation)
  3. Direct blocking of ACh binding to AChR

This pathophysiology creates two actionable therapeutic targets:

  • FcRn inhibition (Rystiggo, Vyvgart, Imaavy): Reduces circulating IgG including pathogenic autoantibodies by blocking the FcRn receptor that recycles IgG, accelerating IgG catabolism
  • Complement C5 inhibition (Zilbrysq, Soliris, Ultomiris): Blocks terminal complement activation, preventing MAC-mediated destruction of the endplate

MuSK+ gMG: MuSK autoantibodies (primarily IgG4) do not activate complement. Instead, they directly interfere with MuSK-LRP4 interaction, disrupting AChR clustering. This is why complement inhibitors (Zilbrysq, Soliris, Ultomiris) are NOT indicated for MuSK+ MG — the pathogenic mechanism doesn't involve complement. Only FcRn inhibitors and B-cell depletion therapies are mechanistically rational for MuSK+ patients.

1.4 Standard Treatment Algorithm

gMG Diagnosis (MGFA Class II-IVa)
  |
  v
Symptomatic therapy: Pyridostigmine (Mestinon) — first-line for all patients
  → Provides symptomatic relief but does not address underlying autoimmunity
  |
  v
Conventional immunosuppressive therapy (IST):
  ├── Corticosteroids (prednisone) — rapid onset, significant long-term side effects
  ├── Azathioprine (Imuran) — steroid-sparing, slow onset (6-12 months)
  ├── Mycophenolate mofetil (CellCept) — steroid-sparing, moderate onset
  └── Cyclosporine, tacrolimus — less commonly used
  |
  v
Inadequate response or intolerable IST side effects
  |
  v
Targeted biologics (rapidly evolving landscape — 2023-2026):
  |
  ├── FcRn Inhibitors:
  |     ├── VYVGART / VYVGART HYTRULO (efgartigimod) — argenx, first-to-market, market leader
  |     ├── RYSTIGGO (rozanolixizumab) — UCB, monthly SC infusion cycles
  |     └── IMAAVY (nipocalimab) — J&J, every-2-weeks IV, broadest label (12+ years)
  |
  ├── Complement C5 Inhibitors:
  |     ├── SOLIRIS (eculizumab) — Alexion/AZ, legacy IV, biosimilars arriving
  |     ├── ULTOMIRIS (ravulizumab) — Alexion/AZ, longer-acting IV
  |     └── ZILBRYSQ (zilucoplan) — UCB, daily SC self-injection
  |
  └── B-Cell Depletion:
        ├── Rituximab (off-label, anti-CD20) — widely used, especially for MuSK+
        └── UPLIZNA (inebilizumab) — Amgen, anti-CD19, FDA approved Dec 2025

Acute exacerbation / myasthenic crisis:
  ├── IVIg (intravenous immunoglobulin)
  ├── Plasma exchange (PLEX)
  └── Supportive care (ICU, mechanical ventilation if Class V)

1.5 Unmet Needs

  1. Steroid dependence: Many gMG patients remain on chronic corticosteroids despite targeted biologics. Steroid-sparing potential is a key differentiator for new therapies — Uplizna incorporated a steroid taper into its Phase 3 design
  2. AChR-negative populations: MuSK+ patients have fewer approved options; seronegative patients have NONE (pending Vyvgart sBLA, PDUFA May 2026)
  3. Convenience and self-administration: Frequent infusion center visits burden patients; therapies that enable home self-administration have a structural advantage
  4. Durability vs. continuous dosing: Current FcRn inhibitors require ongoing cyclic treatment; a therapy that induces lasting remission (e.g., B-cell depletion, CAR-T) could transform the paradigm
  5. Treatment sequencing: No consensus guidelines on which biologic class to use first or when to switch — this is where real-world prescribing studies (like ZoomRx's HCP-Pt work) are most valuable
  6. Cost and access: Targeted biologics are expensive ($200K-$500K/year); prior authorization and payer variability create access barriers, especially for newer agents

2. Product Profiles

2.1 RYSTIGGO (rozanolixizumab-noli) — UCB

Mechanism of Action: Rozanolixizumab is a humanized IgG4 monoclonal antibody that binds to the neonatal Fc receptor (FcRn) and inhibits the recycling of IgG. By blocking FcRn-mediated IgG rescue from lysosomal degradation, rozanolixizumab reduces circulating IgG levels — including pathogenic autoantibodies — by up to 60-70% from baseline. This is the same mechanism class as argenx's Vyvgart (efgartigimod) and J&J's Imaavy (nipocalimab).

Approved Indications:

  • US FDA (June 2023): gMG in adult patients who are anti-AChR or anti-MuSK antibody positive
  • EU EC (November 2023): gMG in adults
  • Japan PMDA (March 2024): gMG in adults
  • First and only FDA-approved therapy for BOTH AChR+ and MuSK+ gMG at the time of approval

Dosing and Administration:

  • Subcutaneous infusion, administered over ~15-30 minutes via infusion pump
  • Treatment cycles: 6 weekly infusions followed by a treatment-free interval
  • Cyclic dosing pattern: ~6 weeks on, ~4-9 weeks off (retreatment based on clinical need)
  • Administered under medical supervision (not self-administered at initial approval)
  • EU approval (Jan 2025): Self-administration via infusion pump or new manual push syringe method
  • Japan approval (May 2025): Home self-administration with infusion pump or manual push syringe

Key Efficacy Data (MycarinG Phase 3):

  • Design: Randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled, 81 sites across Asia, Europe, North America
  • Population: AChR+ or MuSK+ gMG, MGFA Class II-IVa, MG-ADL ≥3 (non-ocular), QMG ≥11
  • N=200, randomized 1:1:1 to rozanolixizumab 7 mg/kg, 10 mg/kg, or placebo weekly SC for 6 weeks
  • Primary endpoint (MG-ADL change at Day 43):
    • 7 mg/kg: LS mean change -3.37 (vs. placebo -0.78; difference -2.59; p<0.0001)
    • 10 mg/kg: LS mean change -3.40 (vs. placebo -0.78; difference -2.62; p<0.0001)
  • QMG (secondary):
    • 7 mg/kg: -5.4 points vs. placebo -1.9 (p<0.001)
    • 10 mg/kg: -6.7 points vs. placebo -1.9 (p<0.001)
  • Open-label extension (MG0004): Safety and efficacy of chronic weekly dosing confirmed with sustained improvements

Safety Profile:

  • TEAEs: 81% (7 mg/kg), 83% (10 mg/kg) vs. 67% (placebo)
  • Headache: 45% (7 mg/kg), 38% (10 mg/kg) vs. 19% (placebo) — the hallmark AE
  • Diarrhea, pyrexia, infections, hypersensitivity, nausea reported
  • Boxed warning: None
  • Key safety concern: Infection risk (reduced IgG levels may increase susceptibility to infections)

Patient Support: UCB ONWARD program provides copay assistance, insurance navigation, and access support.

2.2 ZILBRYSQ (zilucoplan) — UCB

Mechanism of Action: Zilucoplan is a macrocyclic peptide that binds to complement component C5, preventing its cleavage into C5a and C5b by C5 convertases. This blocks terminal complement activation and membrane attack complex (MAC) formation, thereby preventing complement-mediated destruction of the neuromuscular junction endplate. This is the same mechanism class as Soliris (eculizumab) and Ultomiris (ravulizumab), but with key formulation differences.

Approved Indications:

  • US FDA (October 2023): gMG in adult patients who are anti-AChR antibody positive
  • EU, Japan, Canada also approved
  • Important limitation: NOT approved for MuSK+ gMG (MuSK autoantibodies are IgG4 and do not activate complement, making C5 inhibition mechanistically irrelevant for this subtype)

Dosing and Administration:

  • Once-daily subcutaneous self-injection (0.3 mg/kg)
  • Self-administered by patients at home after training — first and only gMG biologic with daily self-administration at launch
  • Pre-filled pen approved in EU (CHMP positive opinion received)
  • No infusion center visits required
  • Requires meningococcal vaccination before initiation (as with all C5 inhibitors)

Key Efficacy Data (RAISE Phase 3):

  • Design: Randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled, 12-week treatment
  • Population: AChR+ gMG adults
  • N=174, randomized 1:1 to zilucoplan 0.3 mg/kg SC daily or placebo
  • Primary endpoint (MG-ADL change at Week 12):
    • Zilucoplan: LS mean change -3.37 vs. placebo -1.27 (difference -2.09; p<0.0001)
  • QMG (secondary):
    • Zilucoplan: LS mean change -6.19 vs. placebo -3.25 (difference -2.94; p<0.0001)
  • Rapid onset: Improvement observed as early as Week 1
  • Steroid-sparing: Subgroup analyses show durable, steroid-sparing benefits
  • Complement inhibition maintained even with concomitant IVIg or PLEX

Safety Profile:

  • TEAEs: 77% (zilucoplan) — favorable profile
  • Injection-site bruising: 16%
  • Headache: 15%
  • Diarrhea: 10%
  • MG worsening: 10%
  • Key risk: Meningococcal infection (Neisseria meningitidis) — mandatory vaccination before initiation; boxed warning for serious meningococcal infections
  • Complement blockade may increase susceptibility to encapsulated bacterial infections

UCB's Dual-Brand Strategy: UCB is the only company with TWO approved gMG biologics from DIFFERENT mechanism classes. The strategic logic:

  1. Mechanism choice within one company: Physicians who want FcRn inhibition choose Rystiggo; those who prefer complement C5 inhibition choose Zilbrysq. Either way, UCB wins.
  2. Administration differentiation: Rystiggo = periodic SC infusion cycles (6 weeks on, then off). Zilbrysq = continuous daily SC self-injection. Different patient lifestyles suit different models.
  3. Antibody-subtype coverage: Rystiggo covers AChR+ AND MuSK+. Zilbrysq covers AChR+ only. UCB has the MuSK+ segment locked in through Rystiggo.
  4. Portfolio resilience: If the FcRn market becomes commoditized (Vyvgart, Imaavy, Rystiggo all competing), Zilbrysq offers differentiated positioning as a non-FcRn option.

2.3 UCB Financial Performance (gMG Franchise)

  • FY 2025: Rystiggo + Zilbrysq combined revenue exceeded EUR 270 million
  • UCB total revenue: EUR 7.7 billion (+26% YoY); UCB's growth primarily driven by Bimzelx (>EUR 2.2B), but gMG franchise is a meaningful growth contributor
  • 2026 guidance: Revenue growth expected in high single-digit to low double-digit percentage range at CER
  • Analyst estimate for Zilbrysq 2026: ~$376 million (Evaluate)
  • Market forecast for Zilbrysq through 2030: Projected CAGR of 28.4% to ~$680 million by 2030 (Grand View Research)
  • Rystiggo market forecast through 2030: Growing, with self-administration approvals (EU, Japan) expected to accelerate uptake

3. Competitive Landscape

3.1 FcRn Inhibitor Competitors

VYVGART / VYVGART HYTRULO (efgartigimod) — argenx

The dominant market leader.

  • Mechanism: Humanized IgG1 Fc fragment, engineered for high-affinity FcRn binding
  • Approval: US FDA December 2021 (IV), June 2023 (SC Hytrulo), June 2024 (CIDP expansion); AChR+ gMG only
  • Dosing: VYVGART IV: 10 mg/kg infusion over 1 hour, weekly x4 (1 cycle), retreatment based on clinical need. VYVGART Hytrulo: 1008 mg fixed SC dose, ~30 seconds via pre-filled syringe, weekly x4
  • Revenue: $4.15 billion in 2025 (+90% YoY) — across gMG, CIDP, and ITP (Japan)
  • Market position: >40% of biologic-treated gMG patients on an efgartigimod formulation. First-to-market advantage in FcRn class. Category creator.
  • Pipeline expansion: sBLA for seronegative gMG under FDA review (PDUFA May 10, 2026). If approved, Vyvgart would be the ONLY biologic for seronegative gMG — a ~15% untapped MG population
  • Competitive advantage: Pre-filled syringe for self-injection (20-30 seconds), broad clinical data (ADAPT, ADAPT-SC, ADAPT SERON), CIDP indication expands total addressable market, brand momentum and physician familiarity
  • Competitive vulnerability: AChR+ only label (for now); cyclic dosing means patients have treatment-free intervals with potential symptom recurrence

IMAAVY (nipocalimab-aahu) — Johnson & Johnson

The newest FcRn inhibitor — broadest label.

  • Mechanism: Fully human, aglycosylated IgG1 monoclonal anti-FcRn antibody
  • Approval: US FDA April 30, 2025; EU EC December 2025
  • Dosing: IV infusion every 2 weeks, continuously (no cycle-off period). 30 minutes for first dose, then 15 minutes
  • Patient population: AChR+ or MuSK+ gMG in patients aged 12+ — first and only FcRn inhibitor approved for pediatric gMG (12+)
  • Clinical data (Vivacity-MG3): Superior MG-ADL improvement vs. placebo through 24 weeks (longest primary endpoint for an FcRn inhibitor in gMG); IgG reduction of up to 75% from first dose sustained over 24 weeks; over 2 years of sustained disease control in long-term extension
  • Competitive advantage: Continuous every-2-week dosing (no cycle-off, no symptom rebound), broadest label (AChR+ AND MuSK+, age 12+), consistent IgG suppression
  • Competitive vulnerability: IV-only (requires infusion center), newest to market (limited real-world track record), entering crowded FcRn space

Imaavy is already appearing in ZoomRx's HCP-Pt conversation data (Aug 2025 check-in). HCPs are mentioning Imaavy alongside Vyvgart, Rystiggo, and Zilbrysq as emerging options, which signals rapid awareness and consideration.

3.2 Complement C5 Inhibitor Competitors

SOLIRIS (eculizumab) — Alexion/AstraZeneca

  • Mechanism: Humanized monoclonal anti-C5 antibody
  • Approval: gMG (AChR+ only) in 2017
  • Dosing: IV infusion every 2 weeks after loading period; requires infusion center visits
  • Revenue: ~$2.58 billion globally in 2024 (declining 14% YoY as Ultomiris cannibalization continues)
  • Biosimilars: Amgen's Bkemv (launched March 2025, ~10% discount) and Teva's Epysqli (launched April 2025, ~30% discount) now available for gMG, PNH, aHUS
  • Competitive position: Legacy standard but actively being displaced by Ultomiris (same company, better dosing) and by biosimilar pressure

ULTOMIRIS (ravulizumab) — Alexion/AstraZeneca

  • Mechanism: Long-acting anti-C5 monoclonal antibody (engineered eculizumab with extended half-life)
  • Approval: gMG (AChR+ only) in 2022
  • Dosing: IV infusion every 8 weeks after loading (vs. every 2 weeks for Soliris) — significant convenience improvement
  • Revenue: ~$3.9 billion globally in 2024 (+34% YoY); actively replacing Soliris within Alexion portfolio
  • Competitive position with Zilbrysq: Both are C5 inhibitors for AChR+ gMG. Ultomiris = every-8-week IV infusion. Zilbrysq = daily SC self-injection. Trade-off is frequency vs. site-of-care convenience.

3.3 Emerging Competitors

UPLIZNA (inebilizumab-cdon) — Amgen

  • Mechanism: Anti-CD19 monoclonal antibody causing targeted B-cell depletion (including plasmablasts and some plasma cells — deeper depletion than anti-CD20)
  • Approval: FDA December 12, 2025, for AChR+ and MuSK+ gMG in adults
  • Dosing: IV infusion, two loading doses then every 6 months — just two maintenance doses per year
  • Clinical data (MINT Phase 3): Largest Phase 3 biologic study to include both AChR+ and MuSK+ patients; first to successfully incorporate a steroid taper protocol
  • Competitive threat: "Two doses a year" is an extremely compelling convenience story. If Uplizna achieves meaningful steroid-free remission rates, it could challenge the entire FcRn/C5 paradigm by offering a fundamentally different treatment approach — resetting the immune system rather than managing IgG or complement levels continuously

Cemdisiran (+ Pozelimab) — Regeneron

  • Mechanism: Cemdisiran = C5-targeting siRNA (gene silencing of C5 production); Pozelimab = anti-C5 monoclonal antibody. Can be used as monotherapy or combination
  • Stage: Phase 3 NIMBLE trial positive results (August 2025); regulatory submission planned Q1 2026
  • Dosing: Cemdisiran SC every 12 weeks — quarterly dosing is best-in-class for C5 inhibition
  • Competitive threat to Zilbrysq: If cemdisiran offers similar C5 inhibition with quarterly dosing vs. Zilbrysq's daily injection, the convenience gap is dramatic. This is the biggest future competitive risk to Zilbrysq.

Gefurulimab — AstraZeneca/Alexion

  • Mechanism: Anti-C5 monoclonal antibody (next-gen within Alexion portfolio)
  • Stage: Phase 3 positive results; subcutaneous every-4-week dosing
  • Competitive threat: Addresses Zilbrysq's daily dosing burden with monthly SC injections

IMVT-1402 — Immunovant

  • Mechanism: Next-generation anti-FcRn monoclonal antibody
  • Stage: Phase 3 in gMG
  • Dosing: SC, less frequent than current FcRn inhibitors
  • Competitive threat: If IMVT-1402 achieves deeper or more selective IgG reduction with less frequent dosing, it could challenge Rystiggo's positioning

CAR-T Therapies (Descartes-08, etc.)

  • Mechanism: Anti-BCMA CAR-T (Descartes-08 by Cartesian Therapeutics/Novartis) — one-time treatment aiming for deep B-cell depletion and potentially durable remission
  • Stage: Late-phase development
  • Competitive threat: If CAR-T can induce long-term remission with a single infusion, it would be transformative but likely reserved for refractory patients initially. Long-term competitive threat to all chronic therapies

3.4 Competitive Summary Matrix

Product Company Mechanism Subtype Route Frequency Self-Admin 2025 Revenue
Vyvgart Hytrulo argenx FcRn AChR+ SC (PFS) Weekly x4, cyclic Yes $4.15B (total)
Rystiggo UCB FcRn AChR+ / MuSK+ SC infusion Weekly x6, cyclic EU/Japan ~$270M combined
Imaavy J&J FcRn AChR+ / MuSK+ (12+) IV Every 2 weeks No Launched Apr 2025
Zilbrysq UCB C5 peptide AChR+ SC Daily Yes ~$270M combined
Ultomiris Alexion/AZ C5 mAb AChR+ IV Every 8 weeks No $3.9B (all indications)
Soliris Alexion/AZ C5 mAb AChR+ IV Every 2 weeks No $2.58B (declining)
Uplizna Amgen CD19 B-cell AChR+ / MuSK+ IV Every 6 months No Approved Dec 2025
Cemdisiran Regeneron C5 siRNA TBD SC Every 12 weeks TBD Filing Q1 2026

4. ZoomRx Engagement History

4.1 Active Study: HCP-Patient Conversations (gMG)

Study type: HCP-Patient conversation recordings Client contacts: Rich Durante (Principal), Vincent Valant (Product Manager), Cherie Stuntz (UCB team) ZoomRx team: Sneha Shah (Senior Manager), Ayush Shukla (Engagement Manager), Alexander Colborn (Engagement Manager), Mahima Khandelwal (Consultant) Study value: $90,000 (25 conversations at ~$3,600/conversation after 10% pilot discount) + optional $15,000 for 8 follow-up IDIs

Study design:

  • ~40 total HCP-Patient conversations from 20-22 unique neurologists
  • Minimum 15-25 conversations with Rystiggo/Zilbrysq mentions
  • Neutral, non-directive prompt to avoid biasing brand mentions
  • Target: Neurologists/NP-PAs with ≥5 gMG patients, 2-35 years experience
  • UCB target list representation: 50-60%

Phase 1 (kicked off April 2025, fielded June-November 2025):

  • 22 conversations collected from 12 unique HCPs
  • General Neurologists: 8 conversations; Neuromuscular specialists: 14
  • Product mentions (Phase 1): Rystiggo=8, Zilbrysq=3, Vyvgart=18, Imaavy=9
  • Product prescriptions (Phase 1): Rystiggo=2, Zilbrysq=0, Vyvgart=8, Imaavy=3
  • Recruitment was challenging due to disease rarity (gMG) — 33% of target at mid-fielding

Phase 2 (kicked off January 2026, fielded Feb-April 2026):

  • 18 additional conversations collected, total now 40
  • Product mentions (Phase 2): Rystiggo=5, Zilbrysq=1, Vyvgart=13, Uplizna=5, Imaavy=3
  • Product prescriptions (Phase 2): Rystiggo=1, Zilbrysq=0, Vyvgart=9, Uplizna=3, Imaavy=0
  • Final report delivery targeted for May 2026

4.2 Key Findings from ZoomRx Data (Pre-Final Report)

Vyvgart dominance is overwhelming:

  • Across all 40 conversations, Vyvgart (IV+Hytrulo) had 31 mentions and 17 prescriptions — more than all competitors combined
  • HCPs position Vyvgart as first-line targeted biologic: "first line is steroids... then Rituxan... then of the branded lot, I'm going to VYVGART" (Neurology, 40 gMG patients)
  • Vyvgart pre-filled syringe further strengthens its convenience advantage

Rystiggo is being positioned but not prescribed at volume:

  • 13 total mentions, only 3 prescriptions across 40 conversations
  • HCPs describe Rystiggo positively: "good medicine... targets FcRn receptors... fifteen minutes" but patients often end up on Vyvgart or staying on current therapy
  • UCB's ONWARD program is being leveraged for access: "we got you on that onward program, which allows you to get the drug at a cheaper price"
  • Perception: similar to Vyvgart but less proven — "pretty similar to the way the Vyvgart works"

Zilbrysq is barely registering:

  • Only 4 total mentions, ZERO prescriptions across 40 conversations
  • HCPs mention Zilbrysq in lists of options but never as the recommended therapy
  • Daily injection may be a barrier vs. less frequent alternatives

Imaavy entered awareness quickly:

  • 12 total mentions, 3 prescriptions — rapid uptake for a newly launched product (April 2025)
  • HCPs highlighting 15-minute infusion, every-2-week continuous dosing: "fairly easy... infusion every two weeks"
  • One KOL positioned Imaavy as "you never stop doing it... you don't have these stretches where you go four weeks and you start to feel worse"

Uplizna appeared in Phase 2 data:

  • 5 mentions, 3 prescriptions in Phase 2 alone (approved December 2025) — strong early signal

HCP communication style:

  • 11 of 14 analyzed conversations (79%) were consultative (HCP solicits patient input)
  • 3 of 14 (21%) were prescriptive (HCP directs treatment decision)
  • Safety, quality of life, convenience, and cost are top treatment decision drivers
  • Patients are generally well-informed but defer to neurologist recommendations

5. Market Dynamics

5.1 The gMG Market Is Expanding Rapidly

The total gMG biologic market is estimated at $5.9 billion (7MM, 2025) and growing at ~9% CAGR. Key growth drivers:

  1. Earlier biologic adoption: Physicians are moving targeted biologics earlier in the treatment algorithm, reducing time patients spend on conventional IST alone
  2. New approvals expanding treated population: Each new approval (Imaavy, Uplizna) broadens awareness and legitimizes biologics for gMG
  3. Self-administration trends: SC and home-administered options lower the treatment burden threshold, making biologics accessible to patients who would have declined infusion-based therapy
  4. Pipeline depth: 8+ late-stage candidates signal sustained investment and broader coverage

5.2 Key Strategic Questions for UCB

  1. Can UCB take share from Vyvgart? The ZoomRx data suggests Vyvgart's dominance is deeply entrenched. Rystiggo is mentioned but rarely prescribed. UCB's best opportunity may be in patients who fail or are ineligible for Vyvgart, rather than head-to-head competition.

  2. Does Zilbrysq's daily dosing kill its potential? With cemdisiran (quarterly SC) and gefurulimab (monthly SC) in the pipeline, Zilbrysq's daily injection regimen is increasingly uncompetitive on convenience. UCB may need to reposition Zilbrysq around mechanism differentiation (complement vs. FcRn) rather than convenience.

  3. Will Uplizna's "two doses a year" narrative disrupt the FcRn/C5 paradigm? If B-cell depletion achieves comparable efficacy with dramatically less treatment burden, the chronic dosing models of Rystiggo and Zilbrysq become harder to justify.

  4. Is Imaavy the biggest near-term threat? J&J's commercial capabilities + continuous dosing (no cycle-off symptom rebound) + broadest label make Imaavy a formidable competitor. The ZoomRx data shows rapid HCP awareness even before commercial promotion at scale.

  5. What is UCB's gMG franchise worth in 3-5 years? Combined revenue of EUR 270M is respectable but small vs. Vyvgart ($4.15B). Can UCB reach $1B+ in gMG, or are they destined to be second-tier players behind argenx?

headings.text:
1. Disease Landscape
1.1 Epidemiology
Myasthenia Gravis (MG):

MG is a chronic autoimmune neuromuscular disorder characterized by fluctuating skeletal muscle weakness and fatigue
US prevalence: ~37 cases per 100,000 people (claims-based, 2016-2021 data), equating to ~120,000-150,000 patients in the US
Age- and sex-standardized incidence: ~68.5 new cases per million person-years in commercially/Medicare-insured US populations
Global prevalence estimates range from 20 to 475 per million, with a mean of ~173 per million; rising steadily due to improved diagnosis and aging populations
Bimodal age distribution: early peak in women (20-40 years) and late peak in men (60-80 years); elderly-onset MG is the fastest-growing demographic segment

Generalized MG (gMG):

~50% of patients initially present with ocular symptoms (ptosis, diplopia); of these, ~50% progress to generalized disease within 2 years
gMG involves weakness of limb, bulbar (chewing, swallowing, speaking), and respiratory muscles
MGFA classification: Class I (ocular only) through Class V (intubation with or without mechanical ventilation)
The commercially relevant population for targeted biologics is Class II-IVa gMG — this is the population studied in pivotal trials

1.2 MG Subtypes by Autoantibody
MG is classified by the pathogenic autoantibody, which has direct implications for treatment eligibility:



Subtype
Prevalence
Target
Approved Biologics




AChR Ab+
80-85% of MG
Acetylcholine receptor at the neuromuscular junction
Vyvgart, Rystiggo, Zilbrysq, Soliris, Ultomiris, Imaavy, Uplizna


MuSK Ab+
5-8% of MG
Muscle-specific tyrosine kinase
Vyvgart, Rystiggo, Imaavy, Uplizna


LRP4 Ab+
1-2% of MG
Lipoprotein-related protein 4
No approved biologics (cell-based assay detection only)


Seronegative
10-15% of MG
Unknown / undetectable by standard assays
No approved biologics (Vyvgart sBLA under FDA review, PDUFA May 2026)



Key implication for UCB: Rystiggo is approved for both AChR+ and MuSK+ gMG. Zilbrysq is approved only for AChR+ gMG. This means Rystiggo covers both major antibody subtypes, while Zilbrysq serves the larger AChR+ majority. UCB can offer mechanism choice to AChR+ patients and has a MuSK+ option that competitors like Zilbrysq and Soliris/Ultomiris cannot match.
1.3 Pathophysiology
AChR+ gMG: Autoantibodies (primarily IgG1 and IgG3) bind the acetylcholine receptor at the postsynaptic neuromuscular junction, causing:

Complement-mediated destruction of the endplate (MAC formation)
Accelerated internalization and degradation of AChR (antigenic modulation)
Direct blocking of ACh binding to AChR

This pathophysiology creates two actionable therapeutic targets:

FcRn inhibition (Rystiggo, Vyvgart, Imaavy): Reduces circulating IgG including pathogenic autoantibodies by blocking the FcRn receptor that recycles IgG, accelerating IgG catabolism
Complement C5 inhibition (Zilbrysq, Soliris, Ultomiris): Blocks terminal complement activation, preventing MAC-mediated destruction of the endplate

MuSK+ gMG: MuSK autoantibodies (primarily IgG4) do not activate complement. Instead, they directly interfere with MuSK-LRP4 interaction, disrupting AChR clustering. This is why complement inhibitors (Zilbrysq, Soliris, Ultomiris) are NOT indicated for MuSK+ MG — the pathogenic mechanism doesn't involve complement. Only FcRn inhibitors and B-cell depletion therapies are mechanistically rational for MuSK+ patients.
1.4 Standard Treatment Algorithm
gMG Diagnosis (MGFA Class II-IVa)
  |
  v
Symptomatic therapy: Pyridostigmine (Mestinon) — first-line for all patients
  → Provides symptomatic relief but does not address underlying autoimmunity
  |
  v
Conventional immunosuppressive therapy (IST):
  ├── Corticosteroids (prednisone) — rapid onset, significant long-term side effects
  ├── Azathioprine (Imuran) — steroid-sparing, slow onset (6-12 months)
  ├── Mycophenolate mofetil (CellCept) — steroid-sparing, moderate onset
  └── Cyclosporine, tacrolimus — less commonly used
  |
  v
Inadequate response or intolerable IST side effects
  |
  v
Targeted biologics (rapidly evolving landscape — 2023-2026):
  |
  ├── FcRn Inhibitors:
  |     ├── VYVGART / VYVGART HYTRULO (efgartigimod) — argenx, first-to-market, market leader
  |     ├── RYSTIGGO (rozanolixizumab) — UCB, monthly SC infusion cycles
  |     └── IMAAVY (nipocalimab) — J&J, every-2-weeks IV, broadest label (12+ years)
  |
  ├── Complement C5 Inhibitors:
  |     ├── SOLIRIS (eculizumab) — Alexion/AZ, legacy IV, biosimilars arriving
  |     ├── ULTOMIRIS (ravulizumab) — Alexion/AZ, longer-acting IV
  |     └── ZILBRYSQ (zilucoplan) — UCB, daily SC self-injection
  |
  └── B-Cell Depletion:
        ├── Rituximab (off-label, anti-CD20) — widely used, especially for MuSK+
        └── UPLIZNA (inebilizumab) — Amgen, anti-CD19, FDA approved Dec 2025

Acute exacerbation / myasthenic crisis:
  ├── IVIg (intravenous immunoglobulin)
  ├── Plasma exchange (PLEX)
  └── Supportive care (ICU, mechanical ventilation if Class V)

1.5 Unmet Needs

Steroid dependence: Many gMG patients remain on chronic corticosteroids despite targeted biologics. Steroid-sparing potential is a key differentiator for new therapies — Uplizna incorporated a steroid taper into its Phase 3 design
AChR-negative populations: MuSK+ patients have fewer approved options; seronegative patients have NONE (pending Vyvgart sBLA, PDUFA May 2026)
Convenience and self-administration: Frequent infusion center visits burden patients; therapies that enable home self-administration have a structural advantage
Durability vs. continuous dosing: Current FcRn inhibitors require ongoing cyclic treatment; a therapy that induces lasting remission (e.g., B-cell depletion, CAR-T) could transform the paradigm
Treatment sequencing: No consensus guidelines on which biologic class to use first or when to switch — this is where real-world prescribing studies (like ZoomRx's HCP-Pt work) are most valuable
Cost and access: Targeted biologics are expensive ($200K-$500K/year); prior authorization and payer variability create access barriers, especially for newer agents


2. Product Profiles
2.1 RYSTIGGO (rozanolixizumab-noli) — UCB
Mechanism of Action:
Rozanolixizumab is a humanized IgG4 monoclonal antibody that binds to the neonatal Fc receptor (FcRn) and inhibits the recycling of IgG. By blocking FcRn-mediated IgG rescue from lysosomal degradation, rozanolixizumab reduces circulating IgG levels — including pathogenic autoantibodies — by up to 60-70% from baseline. This is the same mechanism class as argenx's Vyvgart (efgartigimod) and J&J's Imaavy (nipocalimab).
Approved Indications:

US FDA (June 2023): gMG in adult patients who are anti-AChR or anti-MuSK antibody positive
EU EC (November 2023): gMG in adults
Japan PMDA (March 2024): gMG in adults
First and only FDA-approved therapy for BOTH AChR+ and MuSK+ gMG at the time of approval

Dosing and Administration:

Subcutaneous infusion, administered over ~15-30 minutes via infusion pump
Treatment cycles: 6 weekly infusions followed by a treatment-free interval
Cyclic dosing pattern: ~6 weeks on, ~4-9 weeks off (retreatment based on clinical need)
Administered under medical supervision (not self-administered at initial approval)
EU approval (Jan 2025): Self-administration via infusion pump or new manual push syringe method
Japan approval (May 2025): Home self-administration with infusion pump or manual push syringe

Key Efficacy Data (MycarinG Phase 3):

Design: Randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled, 81 sites across Asia, Europe, North America
Population: AChR+ or MuSK+ gMG, MGFA Class II-IVa, MG-ADL ≥3 (non-ocular), QMG ≥11
N=200, randomized 1:1:1 to rozanolixizumab 7 mg/kg, 10 mg/kg, or placebo weekly SC for 6 weeks
Primary endpoint (MG-ADL change at Day 43):

7 mg/kg: LS mean change -3.37 (vs. placebo -0.78; difference -2.59; p<0.0001)
10 mg/kg: LS mean change -3.40 (vs. placebo -0.78; difference -2.62; p<0.0001)


QMG (secondary):

7 mg/kg: -5.4 points vs. placebo -1.9 (p<0.001)
10 mg/kg: -6.7 points vs. placebo -1.9 (p<0.001)


Open-label extension (MG0004): Safety and efficacy of chronic weekly dosing confirmed with sustained improvements

Safety Profile:

TEAEs: 81% (7 mg/kg), 83% (10 mg/kg) vs. 67% (placebo)
Headache: 45% (7 mg/kg), 38% (10 mg/kg) vs. 19% (placebo) — the hallmark AE
Diarrhea, pyrexia, infections, hypersensitivity, nausea reported
Boxed warning: None
Key safety concern: Infection risk (reduced IgG levels may increase susceptibility to infections)

Patient Support: UCB ONWARD program provides copay assistance, insurance navigation, and access support.
2.2 ZILBRYSQ (zilucoplan) — UCB
Mechanism of Action:
Zilucoplan is a macrocyclic peptide that binds to complement component C5, preventing its cleavage into C5a and C5b by C5 convertases. This blocks terminal complement activation and membrane attack complex (MAC) formation, thereby preventing complement-mediated destruction of the neuromuscular junction endplate. This is the same mechanism class as Soliris (eculizumab) and Ultomiris (ravulizumab), but with key formulation differences.
Approved Indications:

US FDA (October 2023): gMG in adult patients who are anti-AChR antibody positive
EU, Japan, Canada also approved
Important limitation: NOT approved for MuSK+ gMG (MuSK autoantibodies are IgG4 and do not activate complement, making C5 inhibition mechanistically irrelevant for this subtype)

Dosing and Administration:

Once-daily subcutaneous self-injection (0.3 mg/kg)
Self-administered by patients at home after training — first and only gMG biologic with daily self-administration at launch
Pre-filled pen approved in EU (CHMP positive opinion received)
No infusion center visits required
Requires meningococcal vaccination before initiation (as with all C5 inhibitors)

Key Efficacy Data (RAISE Phase 3):

Design: Randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled, 12-week treatment
Population: AChR+ gMG adults
N=174, randomized 1:1 to zilucoplan 0.3 mg/kg SC daily or placebo
Primary endpoint (MG-ADL change at Week 12):

Zilucoplan: LS mean change -3.37 vs. placebo -1.27 (difference -2.09; p<0.0001)


QMG (secondary):

Zilucoplan: LS mean change -6.19 vs. placebo -3.25 (difference -2.94; p<0.0001)


Rapid onset: Improvement observed as early as Week 1
Steroid-sparing: Subgroup analyses show durable, steroid-sparing benefits
Complement inhibition maintained even with concomitant IVIg or PLEX

Safety Profile:

TEAEs: 77% (zilucoplan) — favorable profile
Injection-site bruising: 16%
Headache: 15%
Diarrhea: 10%
MG worsening: 10%
Key risk: Meningococcal infection (Neisseria meningitidis) — mandatory vaccination before initiation; boxed warning for serious meningococcal infections
Complement blockade may increase susceptibility to encapsulated bacterial infections

UCB's Dual-Brand Strategy:
UCB is the only company with TWO approved gMG biologics from DIFFERENT mechanism classes. The strategic logic:

Mechanism choice within one company: Physicians who want FcRn inhibition choose Rystiggo; those who prefer complement C5 inhibition choose Zilbrysq. Either way, UCB wins.
Administration differentiation: Rystiggo = periodic SC infusion cycles (6 weeks on, then off). Zilbrysq = continuous daily SC self-injection. Different patient lifestyles suit different models.
Antibody-subtype coverage: Rystiggo covers AChR+ AND MuSK+. Zilbrysq covers AChR+ only. UCB has the MuSK+ segment locked in through Rystiggo.
Portfolio resilience: If the FcRn market becomes commoditized (Vyvgart, Imaavy, Rystiggo all competing), Zilbrysq offers differentiated positioning as a non-FcRn option.

2.3 UCB Financial Performance (gMG Franchise)

FY 2025: Rystiggo + Zilbrysq combined revenue exceeded EUR 270 million
UCB total revenue: EUR 7.7 billion (+26% YoY); UCB's growth primarily driven by Bimzelx (>EUR 2.2B), but gMG franchise is a meaningful growth contributor
2026 guidance: Revenue growth expected in high single-digit to low double-digit percentage range at CER
Analyst estimate for Zilbrysq 2026: ~$376 million (Evaluate)
Market forecast for Zilbrysq through 2030: Projected CAGR of 28.4% to ~$680 million by 2030 (Grand View Research)
Rystiggo market forecast through 2030: Growing, with self-administration approvals (EU, Japan) expected to accelerate uptake


3. Competitive Landscape
3.1 FcRn Inhibitor Competitors
VYVGART / VYVGART HYTRULO (efgartigimod) — argenx
The dominant market leader.

Mechanism: Humanized IgG1 Fc fragment, engineered for high-affinity FcRn binding
Approval: US FDA December 2021 (IV), June 2023 (SC Hytrulo), June 2024 (CIDP expansion); AChR+ gMG only
Dosing: VYVGART IV: 10 mg/kg infusion over 1 hour, weekly x4 (1 cycle), retreatment based on clinical need. VYVGART Hytrulo: 1008 mg fixed SC dose, ~30 seconds via pre-filled syringe, weekly x4
Revenue: $4.15 billion in 2025 (+90% YoY) — across gMG, CIDP, and ITP (Japan)
Market position: >40% of biologic-treated gMG patients on an efgartigimod formulation. First-to-market advantage in FcRn class. Category creator.
Pipeline expansion: sBLA for seronegative gMG under FDA review (PDUFA May 10, 2026). If approved, Vyvgart would be the ONLY biologic for seronegative gMG — a ~15% untapped MG population
Competitive advantage: Pre-filled syringe for self-injection (20-30 seconds), broad clinical data (ADAPT, ADAPT-SC, ADAPT SERON), CIDP indication expands total addressable market, brand momentum and physician familiarity
Competitive vulnerability: AChR+ only label (for now); cyclic dosing means patients have treatment-free intervals with potential symptom recurrence

IMAAVY (nipocalimab-aahu) — Johnson & Johnson
The newest FcRn inhibitor — broadest label.

Mechanism: Fully human, aglycosylated IgG1 monoclonal anti-FcRn antibody
Approval: US FDA April 30, 2025; EU EC December 2025
Dosing: IV infusion every 2 weeks, continuously (no cycle-off period). 30 minutes for first dose, then 15 minutes
Patient population: AChR+ or MuSK+ gMG in patients aged 12+ — first and only FcRn inhibitor approved for pediatric gMG (12+)
Clinical data (Vivacity-MG3): Superior MG-ADL improvement vs. placebo through 24 weeks (longest primary endpoint for an FcRn inhibitor in gMG); IgG reduction of up to 75% from first dose sustained over 24 weeks; over 2 years of sustained disease control in long-term extension
Competitive advantage: Continuous every-2-week dosing (no cycle-off, no symptom rebound), broadest label (AChR+ AND MuSK+, age 12+), consistent IgG suppression
Competitive vulnerability: IV-only (requires infusion center), newest to market (limited real-world track record), entering crowded FcRn space

Imaavy is already appearing in ZoomRx's HCP-Pt conversation data (Aug 2025 check-in). HCPs are mentioning Imaavy alongside Vyvgart, Rystiggo, and Zilbrysq as emerging options, which signals rapid awareness and consideration.
3.2 Complement C5 Inhibitor Competitors
SOLIRIS (eculizumab) — Alexion/AstraZeneca

Mechanism: Humanized monoclonal anti-C5 antibody
Approval: gMG (AChR+ only) in 2017
Dosing: IV infusion every 2 weeks after loading period; requires infusion center visits
Revenue: ~$2.58 billion globally in 2024 (declining 14% YoY as Ultomiris cannibalization continues)
Biosimilars: Amgen's Bkemv (launched March 2025, ~10% discount) and Teva's Epysqli (launched April 2025, ~30% discount) now available for gMG, PNH, aHUS
Competitive position: Legacy standard but actively being displaced by Ultomiris (same company, better dosing) and by biosimilar pressure

ULTOMIRIS (ravulizumab) — Alexion/AstraZeneca

Mechanism: Long-acting anti-C5 monoclonal antibody (engineered eculizumab with extended half-life)
Approval: gMG (AChR+ only) in 2022
Dosing: IV infusion every 8 weeks after loading (vs. every 2 weeks for Soliris) — significant convenience improvement
Revenue: ~$3.9 billion globally in 2024 (+34% YoY); actively replacing Soliris within Alexion portfolio
Competitive position with Zilbrysq: Both are C5 inhibitors for AChR+ gMG. Ultomiris = every-8-week IV infusion. Zilbrysq = daily SC self-injection. Trade-off is frequency vs. site-of-care convenience.

3.3 Emerging Competitors
UPLIZNA (inebilizumab-cdon) — Amgen

Mechanism: Anti-CD19 monoclonal antibody causing targeted B-cell depletion (including plasmablasts and some plasma cells — deeper depletion than anti-CD20)
Approval: FDA December 12, 2025, for AChR+ and MuSK+ gMG in adults
Dosing: IV infusion, two loading doses then every 6 months — just two maintenance doses per year
Clinical data (MINT Phase 3): Largest Phase 3 biologic study to include both AChR+ and MuSK+ patients; first to successfully incorporate a steroid taper protocol
Competitive threat: "Two doses a year" is an extremely compelling convenience story. If Uplizna achieves meaningful steroid-free remission rates, it could challenge the entire FcRn/C5 paradigm by offering a fundamentally different treatment approach — resetting the immune system rather than managing IgG or complement levels continuously

Cemdisiran (+ Pozelimab) — Regeneron

Mechanism: Cemdisiran = C5-targeting siRNA (gene silencing of C5 production); Pozelimab = anti-C5 monoclonal antibody. Can be used as monotherapy or combination
Stage: Phase 3 NIMBLE trial positive results (August 2025); regulatory submission planned Q1 2026
Dosing: Cemdisiran SC every 12 weeks — quarterly dosing is best-in-class for C5 inhibition
Competitive threat to Zilbrysq: If cemdisiran offers similar C5 inhibition with quarterly dosing vs. Zilbrysq's daily injection, the convenience gap is dramatic. This is the biggest future competitive risk to Zilbrysq.

Gefurulimab — AstraZeneca/Alexion

Mechanism: Anti-C5 monoclonal antibody (next-gen within Alexion portfolio)
Stage: Phase 3 positive results; subcutaneous every-4-week dosing
Competitive threat: Addresses Zilbrysq's daily dosing burden with monthly SC injections

IMVT-1402 — Immunovant

Mechanism: Next-generation anti-FcRn monoclonal antibody
Stage: Phase 3 in gMG
Dosing: SC, less frequent than current FcRn inhibitors
Competitive threat: If IMVT-1402 achieves deeper or more selective IgG reduction with less frequent dosing, it could challenge Rystiggo's positioning

CAR-T Therapies (Descartes-08, etc.)

Mechanism: Anti-BCMA CAR-T (Descartes-08 by Cartesian Therapeutics/Novartis) — one-time treatment aiming for deep B-cell depletion and potentially durable remission
Stage: Late-phase development
Competitive threat: If CAR-T can induce long-term remission with a single infusion, it would be transformative but likely reserved for refractory patients initially. Long-term competitive threat to all chronic therapies

4. ZoomRx Engagement History
4.1 Active Study: HCP-Patient Conversations (gMG)
Study type: HCP-Patient conversation recordings
Client contacts: Rich Durante (Principal), Vincent Valant (Product Manager), Cherie Stuntz (UCB team)
ZoomRx team: Sneha Shah (Senior Manager), Ayush Shukla (Engagement Manager), Alexander Colborn (Engagement Manager), Mahima Khandelwal (Consultant)
Study value: $90,000 (25 conversations at ~$3,600/conversation after 10% pilot discount) + optional $15,000 for 8 follow-up IDIs
Study design:

~40 total HCP-Patient conversations from 20-22 unique neurologists
Minimum 15-25 conversations with Rystiggo/Zilbrysq mentions
Neutral, non-directive prompt to avoid biasing brand mentions
Target: Neurologists/NP-PAs with ≥5 gMG patients, 2-35 years experience
UCB target list representation: 50-60%

Phase 1 (kicked off April 2025, fielded June-November 2025):

22 conversations collected from 12 unique HCPs
General Neurologists: 8 conversations; Neuromuscular specialists: 14
Product mentions (Phase 1): Rystiggo=8, Zilbrysq=3, Vyvgart=18, Imaavy=9
Product prescriptions (Phase 1): Rystiggo=2, Zilbrysq=0, Vyvgart=8, Imaavy=3
Recruitment was challenging due to disease rarity (gMG) — 33% of target at mid-fielding

Phase 2 (kicked off January 2026, fielded Feb-April 2026):

18 additional conversations collected, total now 40
Product mentions (Phase 2): Rystiggo=5, Zilbrysq=1, Vyvgart=13, Uplizna=5, Imaavy=3
Product prescriptions (Phase 2): Rystiggo=1, Zilbrysq=0, Vyvgart=9, Uplizna=3, Imaavy=0
Final report delivery targeted for May 2026

4.2 Key Findings from ZoomRx Data (Pre-Final Report)
Vyvgart dominance is overwhelming:

Across all 40 conversations, Vyvgart (IV+Hytrulo) had 31 mentions and 17 prescriptions — more than all competitors combined
HCPs position Vyvgart as first-line targeted biologic: "first line is steroids... then Rituxan... then of the branded lot, I'm going to VYVGART" (Neurology, 40 gMG patients)
Vyvgart pre-filled syringe further strengthens its convenience advantage

Rystiggo is being positioned but not prescribed at volume:

13 total mentions, only 3 prescriptions across 40 conversations
HCPs describe Rystiggo positively: "good medicine... targets FcRn receptors... fifteen minutes" but patients often end up on Vyvgart or staying on current therapy
UCB's ONWARD program is being leveraged for access: "we got you on that onward program, which allows you to get the drug at a cheaper price"
Perception: similar to Vyvgart but less proven — "pretty similar to the way the Vyvgart works"

Zilbrysq is barely registering:

Only 4 total mentions, ZERO prescriptions across 40 conversations
HCPs mention Zilbrysq in lists of options but never as the recommended therapy
Daily injection may be a barrier vs. less frequent alternatives

Imaavy entered awareness quickly:

12 total mentions, 3 prescriptions — rapid uptake for a newly launched product (April 2025)
HCPs highlighting 15-minute infusion, every-2-week continuous dosing: "fairly easy... infusion every two weeks"
One KOL positioned Imaavy as "you never stop doing it... you don't have these stretches where you go four weeks and you start to feel worse"

Uplizna appeared in Phase 2 data:

5 mentions, 3 prescriptions in Phase 2 alone (approved December 2025) — strong early signal

HCP communication style:

11 of 14 analyzed conversations (79%) were consultative (HCP solicits patient input)
3 of 14 (21%) were prescriptive (HCP directs treatment decision)
Safety, quality of life, convenience, and cost are top treatment decision drivers
Patients are generally well-informed but defer to neurologist recommendations


5. Market Dynamics
5.1 The gMG Market Is Expanding Rapidly
The total gMG biologic market is estimated at $5.9 billion (7MM, 2025) and growing at ~9% CAGR. Key growth drivers:

Earlier biologic adoption: Physicians are moving targeted biologics earlier in the treatment algorithm, reducing time patients spend on conventional IST alone
New approvals expanding treated population: Each new approval (Imaavy, Uplizna) broadens awareness and legitimizes biologics for gMG
Self-administration trends: SC and home-administered options lower the treatment burden threshold, making biologics accessible to patients who would have declined infusion-based therapy
Pipeline depth: 8+ late-stage candidates signal sustained investment and broader coverage

5.2 Key Strategic Questions for UCB
